Fischerspooner's theatrical spectacle, Peaches' sexually confrontational lyrics, and Miss Kittin's deadpan delivery defined the genre's range. Electroclash was self-consciously stylish and quickly absorbed into the broader indie-electronic world, but its influence on the 2000s electro revival and artists like Crystal Castles was direct.

name: Nu-Disco
description:
Nu-disco revived the warm, string-laden, melodically rich sound of late 1970s disco in an era when electronic music had largely moved toward colder, harder sounds. Where classic disco was made with orchestras and studio musicians, nu-disco uses synthesizers, samples, and electronic production to recreate the aesthetic. The result is dance music that feels emotionally warmer than most club music of the 2000s.

Swedish duo Studio and French artists like Cassius helped establish the sound. Lindstrom from Norway developed a more extended, cosmic version that drew on cosmic disco and Italo. The genre found a home in the same late-night dance spaces that had once housed deep house and is often associated with a fashion-conscious, art-adjacent aesthetic.

name: Future Bass
description:
Future bass is characterized by dense, layered synthesizer chords, pitched-up vocal chops, and a combination of trap-influenced rhythms with electronic pop melody. Where dubstep was dark and aggressive, future bass is bright and emotionally overwhelming -- the production is designed to create the feeling of euphoria through sheer density of sound.

Flume from Australia and Rustie from the UK were early architects. The genre became closely associated with the festival EDM circuit and with online music platforms like SoundCloud, where producers posted tracks directly to audiences without labels. Its emotional directness made it accessible to large youth audiences, and it crossed into mainstream pop production through the mid-2010s.

name: Lo-Fi Hip-Hop
description:
Lo-fi hip-hop uses vinyl crackle, tape hiss, detuned samples from jazz and soul records, and slow, simple drum patterns to create music with a warm, imperfect texture. The tempo is slow, the mood is contemplative, and the production deliberately avoids the polished sound of mainstream hip-hop. It became the defining background music of study sessions, work playlists, and late-night solitude.

Youtube channels streaming 'lo-fi hip-hop beats to study/relax to' with a looping anime image turned the genre into a cultural phenomenon in the mid-2010s. Nujabes's work in the 2000s -- blending jazz samples with hip-hop beats -- is the primary aesthetic ancestor. The genre is now a global phenomenon produced by independent artists in every country.

name: Chillwave
description:
Chillwave applied bedroom production techniques to a synthesizer-based sound that evoked hazy summer nostalgia. The production featured heavy reverb on everything, synthesizers tuned to sound slightly off and warm, and vocals processed until they merged with the instrumentation. It captured a specific digital moment -- late 2000s internet culture, infinite SoundCloud scrolling, a generation raised on 1980s pop memories they were too young to have.

Washed Out, Neon Indian, and Toro y Moi were the genre's central figures, all releasing significant music in 2009 and 2010. Pitchfork critic William Bowers coined the term, and the genre became one of the first to be fully documented and discussed by online music media before it had any significant physical presence. It was a cultural mood as much as a specific sound.

name: Vaporwave
description:
Vaporwave is electronic music made from slowed, pitched-down samples of 1980s and 1990s commercial music: elevator music, smooth jazz, advertising jingles, late-night infomercial background tracks. The production creates music that sounds like hearing a mall from across a parking lot at 3 AM -- familiar but wrong, nostalgic but slightly menacing. Macintosh Plus and James Ferraro released the genre's defining early works in 2011 and 2010.

Vaporwave is also a visual aesthetic of pastel grids, Roman statues, Windows 95 interfaces, and Japanese text. It was the first genre to be born entirely online, with no physical scene. Its critique of consumer capitalism and nostalgia is debated -- some read it as ironic, some as sincere longing. The genre spawned numerous sub-genres including mallsoft, future funk, and hardvapor.

name: Tech House
description:
Tech house combines the functional groove orientation of house music with the mechanical precision and stripped-back production of techno. The tempo sits between the two genres, and the tracks typically feature prominent basslines, minimal vocals or vocal samples, and a rhythmic tightness borrowed from techno layered over house music's warmth. Steve Bug and Cajmere were early architects.

The genre became enormously commercially successful in the 2010s through artists like Fisher and Chris Lake, who took its raw ingredients and built them into festival-ready tracks. Tech house is now one of the dominant sounds of mainstream dance music, though the commercial success has brought criticism from purists who value its original, more underground character.

name: EDM
description:
EDM, as a commercial genre classification, describes the American mainstream electronic dance music market that exploded in popularity around 2010. It draws from trance, electro house, and dubstep and is characterized by large builds, climactic drops, and a production designed for festival stages and arena performances. Deadmau5, David Guetta, and Skrillex were among its primary commercial architects.

EDM is partly a marketing category rather than a specific sound, describing a market audience rather than a single style. The music is optimized for emotional impact on large crowds: the build creates tension, the drop releases it. Ultra Music Festival in Miami and Tomorrowland in Belgium are its primary events. The genre made electronic music commercially central in the United States for the first time.

name: Psytrance
description:
Psytrance is faster, stranger, and more psychedelically disorienting than mainstream trance. Tempo runs from 140 to 150 BPM, the synthesizer work is intricately layered, and the music is designed for outdoor festivals and marathon dance sessions rather than conventional clubs. The Goa scene in India, where European backpackers encountered the music in the late 1980s and early 1990s, gave the genre its original form as Goa trance.

Israeli producers and labels developed the most commercially significant version of psytrance in the mid-1990s. The genre has a distinctive community associated with outdoor gatherings, a countercultural aesthetic, and an intense focus on the physical and psychological effects of the music. Infected Mushroom became the genre's most commercially successful act.

name: Hardstyle
description:
Hardstyle combines the melodic elements of trance with the aggression and high tempo of gabber hardcore. The defining feature is the 'kick' -- a heavily distorted, screeching bass drum sound that punches through every other element of the mix. Tracks typically run between 140 and 150 BPM and alternate between hard, distorted sections and melodic breakdowns with synthesizer leads.

The Dutch scene produced the genre through labels like Scantraxx and artists like Showtek and Headhunterz. Defqon.1 festival in the Netherlands is the genre's largest event. Hardstyle has developed a passionate, dedicated fanbase in the Netherlands, Belgium, and Australia, with less presence in the UK or US mainstream.

name: Footwork
description:
Footwork music was created in Chicago for a specific competitive dance style called footworking, in which dancers perform rapid, precise foot movements at speed. The music reflects this: 160 BPM, heavily syncopated rhythms with multiple patterns overlapping, vocal samples cut into rhythmic fragments, and a physicality that is explicitly tied to bodily movement. DJ Rashad and DJ Spinn were the genre's central figures.

Footwork remained largely unknown outside Chicago until UK label Planet Mu began releasing it to international audiences in 2010. The music attracted electronic music producers worldwide who recognized its rhythmic complexity as something genuinely novel. DJ Rashad's death in 2014 brought both mourning and renewed attention to the form he had helped create.

name: Electro House
description:
Electro house combines the four-on-the-floor kick pattern of house music with the harder, more distorted sounds and bigger production of electro. The basslines are prominent and aggressive, the builds are dramatic, and the overall effect is louder and more aggressive than traditional house. Swedish House Mafia, Justice, and Daft Punk's later work helped establish the sound in the mid-2000s.

The genre became one of the dominant sounds of festival and club culture in the late 2000s. Its commercial peak coincided with the EDM explosion in the US around 2010 to 2013. Justice's cross-shaped LED rig and the maximalist production of Swedish House Mafia set the visual and sonic template for the era.

name: Breakbeat
description:
Breakbeat uses drum patterns based on 'breaks' -- the percussion-only sections of funk and soul records that DJs extended for dancers, as pioneered by DJ Kool Herc. Where hip-hop used breaks as a foundation for rapping, breakbeat dance music built entire tracks around looped or chopped break patterns at faster tempos for club and rave environments. The Amen break and the Think break became the most sampled drum recordings in electronic music history.

Breakbeat hardened into jungle and drum and bass in the early 1990s UK rave scene. A parallel track produced big-beat, breakbeat hardcore, and the 4-bar break patterns that still underlie much electronic music. The genre encompasses enormous rhythmic variety, from the 808-based patterns of hip-hop to the processed, pitched drum work of IDM.

name: Big Beat
description:
Big beat combined breakbeat rhythms with rock and pop influences to create loud, brash, unapologetically fun dance music. The Prodigy, Chemical Brothers, and Fatboy Slim built tracks from massive samples, distorted drums, and a sense of reckless energy that made them work at both raves and mainstream radio. The genre was deliberately unsubtle -- the production was a weapon aimed at maximum impact.

Fatboy Slim's Better Living Through Chemistry and The Prodigy's Music for the Jilted Generation defined the early peak. The genre reached mainstream commercial success in the late 1990s before its energy dissipated into more polished pop-electronic territory. Big beat was the moment electronic music most successfully crossed into rock and pop audiences.

name: Darkwave
description:
Darkwave emerged from post-punk's gothic fringe, combining the cold electronic textures of new wave with an aesthetic of melancholy, romanticism, and the macabre. It differs from goth rock in its greater emphasis on synthesizers and electronic production over live guitars. German band Clan of Xymox and Belgian act Dead Can Dance were among the genre's early architects.

The genre encompasses considerable variety, from the synth-driven coldwave of Xymox to the orchestral, world-music influenced sound of Dead Can Dance to the more aggressive electronic-industrial territory of acts like Wumpscut. It remains a vital underground scene with a strong following in Europe, North America, and South America.
key_artists: Black Tape for a Blue Girl

name: Ambient House
description:
Ambient house applied the production techniques and emotional warmth of house music to the slow, atmospheric world of ambient. The Orb, formed in London in 1988, became the genre's defining act, building tracks from long synthesizer pads, found sounds, and samples over a gentle four-to-the-floor beat that was present but never dominating. Their 40-minute track 'A Huge Ever Growing Pulsating Brain That Rules from the Centre of the Ultraworld' captured the approach.

The genre emerged from the chill-out rooms of acid house raves, where coming-down ravers needed something less intense. KLF's Chill Out album in 1990 was equally influential. Ambient house influenced trip-hop, downtempo, and the broader ambient electronic scene of the 1990s.

name: East Coast Hip-Hop
description:
East Coast hip-hop developed in New York and is characterized by jazz and soul samples, complex lyricism, and a lyrical tradition that prizes wordplay and verbal dexterity above all else. The Bronx, Harlem, Brooklyn, and Queens each produced distinct sounds and voices, but all shared an emphasis on rhyme craft. The DJ's role in constructing beats from samples -- particularly from the Native Tongues collective and later from producers like DJ Premier -- gave the sound its characteristic density.

Nas's Illmatic, Jay-Z's Reasonable Doubt, and Notorious B.I.G.'s Ready to Die are the canonical documents of the genre's golden era. The East Coast-West Coast rivalry of the early 1990s both defined and ultimately damaged the scene. New York hip-hop's emphasis on lyricism and production sophistication has maintained its influence even as commercial dominance shifted to Atlanta.

name: West Coast Hip-Hop
description:
West Coast hip-hop developed primarily in Los Angeles and the surrounding Southern California area. Where New York hip-hop emphasized jazz samples and lyrical density, the West Coast sound was initially built on slower, funk-driven production with a laid-back delivery that reflected the climate and car culture of LA. Dr. Dre's G-funk sound in the early 1990s -- synthesizer leads over slow funk samples and a rolling low-rider beat -- became the genre's most commercially successful and imitated template.

The Los Angeles scene produced some of the most commercially successful hip-hop of any era. Tupac Shakur, Snoop Dogg, and Ice Cube each represented different aspects of the West Coast aesthetic. Kendrick Lamar's emergence from Compton in the 2010s brought a new generation of complexity to the tradition.

name: Southern Hip-Hop
description:
Southern hip-hop encompasses the diverse output from Atlanta, Houston, New Orleans, and other Southern cities, characterized by slower tempos than New York or Los Angeles hip-hop, heavy bass, regional slang, and production that draws from local Black music traditions. Miami bass in the late 1980s was an early form; OutKast and Goodie Mob in Atlanta in the early 1990s defined a more lyrical Southern approach.

Houston's chopped and screwed tradition, pioneered by DJ Screw, slowed records down dramatically to create a syrupy, meditative quality. New Orleans bounce music developed call-and-response party rap. Atlanta trap became the dominant commercial form globally. Southern hip-hop's regional diversity resists a single description but its rhythmic emphases and bass weight are consistent across its variants.

name: Boom Bap
description:
Boom bap is the sonic template of golden era New York hip-hop: the term itself imitates the kick drum and snare sounds that define it. The production relies on looped samples from jazz, soul, and funk records, a hard-hitting snare on beats 2 and 4, and a sparse arrangement that prioritizes space for the rapper. DJ Premier, Pete Rock, and DJ Shadow built the genre's most celebrated beats.

KRS-One coined the term in 1993, but the sound defines hip-hop from roughly 1988 to 1998. The production values were relatively rough by later standards -- the drums were punchy but not polished, the samples unprocessed. Boom bap experienced a significant revival in the 2010s through artists like Joey Bada$$ and Kendrick Lamar, who explicitly connected to the 1990s New York tradition.

name: Drill
description:
Chicago drill emerged from the city's South Side with a sound and lyrical content reflecting the specific violence of that environment. Producers like Young Chop built tracks from eerie synthesizer melodies, menacing minor-key samples, and trap's signature hi-hat patterns. Chief Keef's 2012 breakthrough brought the sound to international attention before he turned 17.

UK drill, which developed in London around 2012, adapted the Chicago template to British speech patterns and the specific conditions of London's housing estates. The dark synthesizer palette, sliding bass notes, and distinctive skipping hi-hat pattern of UK drill became one of the most imitated production styles globally. Pop Smoke brought New York drill to a mainstream audience before his death in 2020.

name: Cloud Rap
description:
Cloud rap slowed trap production to near-ambient tempos and wrapped the vocals in reverb and delay until they sounded ethereal and distant. Where trap was aggressive and assertive, cloud rap was dreamy and introspective. Producers like Clams Casino created atmospheric beats built from washed-out samples, and rappers like Lil B and ASAP Rocky floated over them with a relaxed, often improvised delivery.

The genre developed entirely online, with artists releasing mixtapes freely on Tumblr and SoundCloud before any major label attention. Its DIY production aesthetic and willingness to value mood over technical precision influenced a generation of artists who heard it as the clearest expression of a specific internet-era sensibility.

name: Phonk
description:
Phonk draws from the Memphis rap tradition of the 1990s -- producers like DJ Screw, Three 6 Mafia, and early Gangsta Pat -- and rebuilds their chopped, reversed, and heavily sampled aesthetic with contemporary trap production tools. The result is hazy, bass-heavy, and deliberately retro. Samples from old Memphis recordings are slowed down and layered over 808s and trap drums.

Phonk became closely associated with car culture and racing videos in Russia and Brazil, where the Memphis rap aesthetic resonated for reasons that had nothing to do with the original cultural context. Artists like Soudiere and Kordhell built large international audiences making music explicitly rooted in 1990s Memphis while those original recordings were largely unknown to their audiences.

name: Horrorcore
description:
Horrorcore uses horror film imagery, extreme violence, and supernatural themes as lyrical content, delivered over hip-hop production. The Geto Boys were early practitioners, with Scarface and Willie D describing graphic violence in a style that owed more to slasher films than to street reportage. Gravediggaz, a supergroup involving RZA and Prince Paul, pushed the horror aesthetic further with deliberately absurdist elements.

The genre's relationship to reality is deliberate ambiguity: is it fantastical horror or commentary on real violence? Esham from Detroit pioneered a version connected to industrial and noise that influenced multiple generations of dark hip-hop. Brotha Lynch Hung and Tech N9ne expanded the form in the 1990s. Horrorcore's extreme aesthetics place it permanently on the margins of hip-hop.

name: Crunk
description:
Crunk is energy as genre. Lil Jon and the East Side Boyz built tracks designed to generate maximum collective aggression on a dance floor: distorted synth stabs, thunderous kick drums, call-and-response chants, and Lil Jon's raspy, shouted exhortations to get crunk. The word is a portmanteau of crazy and drunk, and the music is calibrated to that specific emotional state.

Crunk dominated Southern hip-hop clubs through the early 2000s. Lil Jon's production work for multiple artists made the sound commercially omnipresent. The genre's enthusiasm and lack of pretension made it excellent party music and easy to dismiss critically. Ying Yang Twins and Three 6 Mafia developed related but distinct versions. Its influence on the physical, call-and-response energy of later Southern rap is significant.

name: Quiet Storm
description:
Quiet Storm is a radio format and sub-genre of R&B defined by slow, smooth, romantic ballads. The name comes from Smokey Robinson's 1975 song, which DJ Melvin Lindsey at Howard University's WHUR adopted as the title for a late-night radio show in 1976. The format gave slower, more sensual R&B a specific broadcast context and audience.

The music is characterized by lush production, sophisticated chord progressions, and an intimate vocal approach. Luther Vandross, Anita Baker, and Barry White each defined different aspects of the aesthetic. Quiet Storm remained a dominant R&B radio format through the 1980s and into the 1990s, and the songs associated with it form the backbone of adult contemporary R&B.
key_tracks: Can't Get Enough of Your Love

name: Contemporary R&B
description:
Contemporary R&B is the mainstream form of Black American popular music from the mid-1980s onward, characterized by sophisticated electronic production, polished vocals, and lyrics focused on romantic relationships. It absorbed hip-hop production techniques, synthesizer-heavy arrangements, and increasingly elaborate vocal processing as each decade progressed. Whitney Houston and Janet Jackson defined its commercial peak in the late 1980s.

The 1990s version -- driven by Babyface's production, Mary J. Blige's hip-hop soul, and Boyz II Men's harmonies -- was one of the most commercially dominant forms of popular music globally. Beyonce, Usher, and Rihanna each represent later evolutions of the template. Contemporary R&B's production has become increasingly indistinguishable from mainstream pop, which was always its commercial destination.

name: Funk Soul
description:
Funk soul occupies the space between soul music's emotional depth and funk's rhythmic emphasis. It is more groove-driven than pure soul, with a rhythm section that dominates rather than supports, but it retains soul's emphasis on vocal performance and emotional content. James Brown's evolution from soul to funk in the mid-1960s defines the transition; Curtis Mayfield and the Temptations worked in this territory without being purely either genre.

The sound is denser and more physical than soul but less abstract than funk -- the songs still have conventional structures and the vocals are still central. Chaka Khan and Earth Wind and Fire represent the commercial peak of the combination. Funk soul provided the primary source material for British soul revivals and for neo-soul artists in the 1990s.

name: UK Soul
description:
UK soul developed as British artists interpreted American soul and R&B through a distinctly British sensibility. The sound is generally more restrained than its American counterpart, with cleaner production and jazz harmonic influences. Sade's 1984 debut established the template: cool, refined, emotionally precise, and quietly sophisticated without being cold.

Soul II Soul brought a more rhythmic, bass-heavy version in the late 1980s, blending soul vocals with electronic production in a way that influenced a generation. Beverley Knight, Omar, and later Amy Winehouse each extended the tradition. UK soul has consistently produced artists whose technical vocal ability and production sophistication are matched by an understated quality that sets them apart from American mainstream R&B.

name: Honky-Tonk
description:
Honky-tonk is the sound of the working-class roadside bar: a two-step beat, electric guitar and steel guitar, and songs about drinking, cheating, and heartbreak told without sentimentality. Ernest Tubb brought it to the Grand Ole Opry in the 1940s. Hank Williams refined it into one of the most emotionally direct forms of popular music in American history. The songs are compact and devastating.

Honky-tonk was the dominant country style through the late 1940s and 1950s. Its plainspoken approach to human suffering made it the country genre most resistant to pop crossover. Dwight Yoakam and Merle Haggard each represented later generations maintaining the tradition against Nashville's commercial pressure.
key_tracks: I'm So Lonesome I Could Cry

name: Outlaw Country
description:
Outlaw country was a rebellion against the polished Nashville Sound of the 1960s and early 1970s. Artists like Willie Nelson and Waylon Jennings wanted to record in their own way, with their own bands, on their own terms. The music retained country's core values -- stories, twang, steel guitar -- but added rock and roll attitude and a refusal to be commercially managed.

The Wanted: Outlaw compilation in 1976 made the movement commercially viable and critically recognized. Waylon and Willie, Kris Kristofferson, and Tompall Glaser defined its core. Outlaw country was the authenticity countermovement to Nashville's commercial machine and it permanently expanded what country music could sound like.
key_tracks: Mammas Don't Let Your Babies Grow Up to Be Cowboys
Are You Sure Hank Done It This Way
Take This Job and Shove It

name: Country Pop
description:
Country pop softened country music's rougher edges to appeal to a broader audience. The Nashville Sound of the late 1950s, pioneered by producer Chet Atkins, replaced fiddles and steel guitars with string sections and background vocals to make country radio-friendly for pop audiences. Eddy Arnold and Jim Reeves were early stars of the crossover.

Dolly Parton and Kenny Rogers demonstrated in the 1970s and 80s that country-pop artists could be global superstars. The genre's commercial dominance grew through the 1990s with Garth Brooks and Shania Twain, and Taylor Swift's early career represented its most recent major commercial peak. Country-pop is now the genre's default mainstream form.

name: Alt-Country
description:
Alt-country applies the values of independent rock to country music: DIY recording, anti-commercial aesthetics, and a willingness to engage with the tradition honestly rather than commercially. Uncle Tupelo, formed in Belleville, Illinois in 1987, created the template by combining the rawness of hardcore punk with the emotional directness of traditional country. Their 1990 debut No Depression gave the genre one of its alternative names.

Wilco and Son Volt emerged from Uncle Tupelo's dissolution and each developed distinct approaches to the same terrain. The genre encompasses a wide range, from Lucinda Williams's literary approach to Ryan Adams's prolific singer-songwriter output to Gillian Welch's austere acoustic traditionalism. Alt-country maintains that country's best values are those Nashville has most consistently abandoned.

name: Americana
description:
Americana is a genre classification that emerged in the 1990s to describe music rooted in American folk, country, blues, and roots traditions without fitting neatly into any of them. The Americana Music Association, founded in 1999, defined it formally, but the music itself had existed long before anyone named it. It covers everything from Gillian Welch's spare acoustic folk to Drive-By Truckers' southern rock.

The genre values authenticity, storytelling, and musical tradition over commercial formula. It gave a category to artists who were too country for indie rock and too indie for country radio. Emmylou Harris, Townes Van Zandt, and John Prine are considered its spiritual forebears, and their influence on the Americana movement has been direct and explicitly acknowledged.

name: Western Swing
description:
Western swing applied jazz improvisation and big band arrangements to country music instrumentation. Bob Wills and His Texas Playboys created the definitive version in Texas and Oklahoma during the 1930s, combining fiddles and steel guitar with brass sections, jazz solos, and a driving rhythm that made it ideal for dancing. The music had the feel of swing but the material and accent of country.

Milton Brown and his Musical Brownies were equally important early architects. Western swing flourished through the 1940s and declined when bebop and honky-tonk each pulled audiences in different directions. It has been periodically revived, most notably by Asleep at the Wheel, who have maintained the tradition continuously since the early 1970s.

name: Indie Folk
description:
Indie folk applies independent rock's production sensibility and DIY ethos to acoustic folk instrumentation. The sound is spare and intimate, typically featuring acoustic guitar, banjo, or mandolin with minimal production, but connected to a tradition of indie and college rock rather than the urban folk revival. Iron and Wine and Devendra Banhart pioneered the approach in the early 2000s with home recordings that valued imperfection.

Bon Iver's For Emma, Forever Ago, recorded alone in a Wisconsin cabin, became the genre's defining document and mainstream breakthrough. Fleet Foxes, The Head and the Heart, and The Lumineers brought sweeping harmonies and a warmer, more commercially accessible version. Indie folk became one of the dominant sounds of the 2000s alternative scene.

name: Singer-Songwriter
description:
The singer-songwriter tradition centers a single artist writing, performing, and embodying their own material with minimal arrangement. The voice and the words are primary; instrumentation serves rather than competes. Carole King, James Taylor, and Joni Mitchell defined the form in the late 1960s and early 1970s: confessional, melodic, and built for intimate listening rather than dancing.

The genre spans an enormous range of style. Paul Simon's sophistication, Nick Drake's darkness, and Elliott Smith's devastation all fit within it. Contemporary artists like Phoebe Bridgers and Sufjan Stevens work in the same tradition while drawing on a century of additional influences. The singer-songwriter format has proven the most adaptable framework in popular music.

name: Folk Punk
description:
Folk punk combines acoustic folk instrumentation with punk's speed, aggression, and political directness. The Pogues, who formed in London in 1982, created the defining version by playing Irish traditional music at punk tempo with full punk attitude and lyrical content that celebrated hard drinking, romantic failure, and immigrant experience simultaneously. The result was simultaneously irreverent and genuinely rooted in tradition.

Billy Bragg's solo acoustic punk approach provided a different model: one man, one guitar, maximum political content. The genre influenced a specific strain of anti-folk and protest music that values raw delivery over polish. Andrew Jackson Jihad and Pat the Bunny represent the American underground version that developed in the 2000s.

name: Celtic
description:
Celtic music encompasses the traditional music of Ireland, Scotland, Wales, Brittany, Galicia, and other Celtic cultural regions. It is defined by specific instruments -- the uilleann pipes, tin whistle, fiddle, bodhran, concertina, and harp -- and by a modal melodic sensibility that predates the major-minor system of Western classical music. The tradition is oral, with tunes passed between players rather than from notation.

The Irish folk revival of the 1960s, centered around Chieftains and Planxty, codified the tradition and brought it to international attention. The Riverdance production in 1994 introduced traditional Irish step dancing to a global audience. Celtic music's harmonic and rhythmic vocabulary influenced folk rock, new age, and a strain of world music that romanticizes the pre-industrial European past.

name: Dream Folk
description:
Dream folk applies the atmospheric, reverb-heavy production of dream pop to acoustic folk song structures. The vocals are airy and processed, the acoustic guitar is submerged in texture rather than presented dryly, and the mood is ethereal rather than earthbound. Grouper and Julia Holter developed the form in different directions in the late 2000s.

The genre sits at the intersection of the DIY bedroom recording tradition and the acoustic folk revival, sharing personnel and aesthetic values with both. Beach House and Marissa Nadler brought melodic pop songs into the territory. Dream folk is defined by mood as much as instrumentation: it sounds like memory rather than presence.
key_tracks: Heavy Water / I'd Rather Be Sleeping

name: J-Pop
description:
J-pop is the dominant form of Japanese popular music, characterized by high production quality, elaborate idol culture, and a melodic sensibility that blends Western pop structures with distinctly Japanese aesthetics. The term came into use in the late 1980s to describe the polished pop that was replacing City Pop and enka in mainstream Japanese music culture. Hikaru Utada's debut in 1998 brought J-pop to its commercial peak.

The idol system -- in which performers are cultivated by agencies from a young age and managed as much as celebrities as musicians -- is central to J-pop's commercial structure. Groups like AKB48 and SMAP generated enormous fanbases through regular television appearances and choreographed single releases. J-pop's aesthetic has influenced K-pop and East Asian pop broadly.

name: Afropop
description:
Afropop is the broad category of African popular music that blends local traditions with Western pop and R&B structures. It covers a wide geographic and stylistic range, from South African bubblegum in the 1980s to Ugandan Afrobeats in the 2010s, united by accessible song structures, danceable rhythms, and the integration of local languages and musical elements into global pop formats.

The category differs from Afrobeats (the specific Nigerian style) in being geographically broader. Youssou N'Dour, who mixed Senegalese mbalax with pop production, is one of its most internationally recognized figures. The genre's global commercial expansion in the 2010s has made it one of the fastest-growing categories in the music industry.

name: Teen Pop
description:
Teen pop is pop music produced specifically for a teenage market, characterized by simple song structures, romantic themes, and performers who are themselves young and aspirationally relatable. Ricky Nelson in the late 1950s was an early practitioner; the genre found its commercial formula through the Brill Building songwriting machine in the early 1960s, which produced hits for Connie Francis and Bobby Darin.

The 1990s wave -- Britney Spears, *NSYNC, Backstreet Boys, and Hanson -- became one of the most commercially significant moments in pop music history. That generation was followed by Disney Channel acts and later by YouTube and TikTok-discovered artists. Teen pop's production values and marketing approaches have remained consistent despite changing decades.

name: Juju
description:
Juju music is a Yoruba popular music form from southwestern Nigeria built around talking drum percussion, electric guitar, and praise singing. I.K. Dairo developed it as a recognizable commercial genre in the 1950s. King Sunny Ade brought it to international attention in the early 1980s with densely layered productions that incorporated multiple talking drums alongside electric guitars and steel guitar.

The music is characterized by long, gradual builds, call-and-response vocal patterns between the lead singer and chorus, and a rhythmic complexity that draws from Yoruba ceremonial music. Ebenezer Obey developed a more Christian-oriented version called miliki music. Juju remains a central form of celebratory music in Yoruba communities worldwide.

name: Zouk
description:
Zouk is a dance music genre from the French Antilles, specifically Guadeloupe and Martinique, created by the band Kassav in the late 1970s. The music features a steady, driving rhythm derived from kadans and biguine, dense synthesizer arrangements, and French Creole lyrics. The word 'zouk' originally meant party in Guadeloupean Creole.

Kassav's commercial success spread zouk throughout the Caribbean, Cape Verde, Brazil, and Africa, where it generated distinct regional variations. Cape Verdean zouk incorporated local morna and coladera elements. Brazilian zouk, also called lambada zouk, developed into a distinct partner dance and musical tradition. Zouk remains one of the most widely danced social dance genres in the world.
key_tracks: Zouk la sé sèl médikaman nou ni

name: Kizomba
description:
Kizomba developed in Angola in the 1980s as a slower, more intimate version of zouk, blending Caribbean zouk rhythms with Angolan semba and local African influences. The tempo is gentle and the bass is prominent, creating music that is designed for very close partner dancing. Eduardo Paim and Eduardo Ferreira are credited as early key figures in establishing the genre.

Kizomba spread through the Lusophone world -- Portugal, Cape Verde, Guinea-Bissau, Brazil -- before reaching broader European and global dance communities in the 2000s. The dance form, characterized by a close hold and sinuous hip movement, became one of the most popular partner dances in Europe by the 2010s.

name: Afro House
description:
Afro house blends the four-on-the-floor structure of house music with percussion patterns, melodic elements, and vocal styles rooted in sub-Saharan African music traditions. Developed primarily in South Africa, it sits between the Chicago house tradition and the local South African house scene that had been developing since the late 1980s. The South African producers who developed it drew on local township sounds and Afrobeats from West Africa.

Black Coffee became the genre's most internationally recognized artist, bringing it to Ibiza and global festival circuits. The music typically features deep bass, African percussion patterns layered over house rhythms, Zulu or Xhosa vocal samples, and a warmth that distinguishes it from European house styles.

name: Afro Soul
description:
Afro soul blends the emotional depth and vocal emphasis of American soul with African melodic and rhythmic traditions. South African artists like Simphiwe Dana and Lira developed the sound in the early 2000s, singing primarily in isiXhosa and other South African languages over arrangements that incorporated traditional instruments alongside contemporary production. The result carries the warmth of soul music but with a distinctly African tonal and rhythmic character.

The genre is related to but distinct from neo-soul: where neo-soul is a specifically American form drawing on 1970s soul records, afro soul draws on African traditions including mbube, maskanda, and traditional praise singing. The genre has grown in prominence alongside the broader global interest in African popular music.

name: Baile Funk
description:
Baile funk (also called funk carioca) developed in the baile funk dance parties of Rio de Janeiro's favelas in the late 1980s, built initially on Miami bass rhythms imported on tape from the US. Rio producers stripped those rhythms down and built them back up with local sensibility: distorted bass, rapid hi-hats, and a raw, confrontational vocal style called MC-ing that narrated favela life explicitly.

The genre exists outside the formal music industry and outside elite Brazilian culture, representing the favela's self-made entertainment economy. Anitta brought a commercially polished version to international audiences. The genre's most extreme variant, proibidão (forbidden funk), deals explicitly with drug trade content and operates entirely underground. Baile funk's rhythmic DNA has influenced global electronic music and Anitta's international pop.

name: MPB
description:
MPB (Musica Popular Brasileira) is the umbrella term for the Brazilian popular music that emerged after bossa nova and absorbed its sophistication while expanding to include rock, samba, baiao, and political protest. The military dictatorship that took power in 1964 paradoxically created the conditions for some of the most creative music in Brazilian history, as artists used allegory and oblique imagery to comment on political repression.

Calinhos Brown, Caetano Veloso, and Gilberto Gil founded the Tropicalia movement in 1967, which applied bossa nova's harmonic sophistication to psychedelic rock and concrete poetry. Chico Buarque wrote some of the most celebrated protest songs in any language. MPB remains an active and vital tradition, encompassing an enormous range of sounds united by a Brazilian identity and a commitment to musical craft.

name: Forró
description:
Forro is the dance music of northeastern Brazil, built on accordion, zabumba (a large drum), and triangle. Luiz Gonzaga popularized it nationally in the 1940s and 50s, bringing the sound of the Nordeste to urban Brazilians who had migrated to Sao Paulo and Rio. The music combines African rhythmic elements with European accordion traditions in a way that is unmistakably northeastern Brazilian.

The dance form that accompanies forro is close and rhythmically intricate, varying across the sub-styles: baiao, xote, and xaxado each have distinct rhythms and dance patterns. A university forrozinho circuit has kept the music vital among younger Brazilians since the 1990s. Forro remains one of the most beloved regional music forms in Brazil.

name: Axé
description:
Axe music emerged from Salvador, Bahia in the mid-1980s, blending Caribbean rhythms -- zouk, soca, merengue -- with Afro-Brazilian percussion traditions and Rio Carnival energy. The word axé means positive energy or spiritual force in the Yoruba tradition, and the music is explicitly celebratory. Luiz Caldas's 1985 hit 'Fricote' is generally cited as the genre's starting point.

Carlinhos Brown, Ivete Sangalo, and Daniela Mercury became the genre's biggest stars. Axe is most closely associated with Salvador's Carnaval, where enormous trios electricos -- sound-system trucks -- parade through the streets while performers play for crowds of hundreds of thousands. The music's pan-Caribbean influences reflect Bahia's strong African and Caribbean cultural connections.

name: Soca
description:
Soca emerged in Trinidad and Tobago in the mid-1970s as a modernization of calypso, blending it with Indian musical influences and a faster, more dance-oriented energy. Lord Shorty (later Ras Shorty I) coined the name, merging 'soul' and 'calypso.' The music accelerated calypso's tempo, added synthesizers and electronic bass, and shifted the lyrical focus toward the joy of carnival and dancing.

Soca is now the central music of carnival culture throughout the Caribbean. Machel Montano dominated the genre commercially for decades; Bunji Garlin and Faye-Ann Lyons brought a more aggressive dancehall-influenced style. Power soca and groovy soca represent its two main contemporary variants.

name: Calypso
description:
Calypso developed in Trinidad at the turn of the 20th century as a form of social commentary in song. Its origins are in African oral traditions carried by enslaved people, and it was performed in tents during carnival season as competitive singing in which kaiso (calypsonians) traded insults, political satire, and commentary on public events. The form was simultaneously entertainment and journalism.

Harry Belafonte brought calypso to international mainstream audiences in the 1950s with an accessible pop version that made his Calypso album one of the first LPs to sell a million copies. The genre's social commentary tradition has remained alive throughout its commercial evolution. Lord Kitchener and Mighty Sparrow dominated its golden era.

name: Merengue
description:
Merengue is the national music and dance of the Dominican Republic, characterized by a fast 2/4 rhythm, accordion (replaced by saxophone in modern versions), guitar, and the tambora drum. The dance features a distinctive limping step that, according to legend, originated in imitation of a wounded general. It became the Dominican Republic's dominant popular music through the 20th century.

Juan Luis Guerra modernized merengue in the 1980s with sophisticated arrangements and socially aware lyrics, bringing it to international audiences. Wilfrido Vargas and Johnny Ventura had previously brought it out of the countryside and into urban dance halls. Bachata, its slower companion, has since surpassed merengue commercially, but merengue remains central to Dominican cultural identity.

name: Bachata
description:
Bachata developed in the bars and brothels of the Dominican Republic's poor and rural communities, dismissed by the urban middle class as the music of poverty and low social status. The name itself was a slang term for a backyard party. It features a small guitar ensemble -- tres, bass guitar, guitar, and guira -- playing romantic songs about heartbreak and longing, with a bittersweet guitar tone produced by heavy guitar bends.

Juan Luis Guerra's 1990 album Bachata Rosa brought the genre mainstream respectability and international audiences. Romeo Santos transformed bachata into one of the most commercially successful Latin genres of the 21st century, blending it with R&B and hip-hop. The dance form became one of the most popular in Latin social dance circles globally.

name: Kuduro
description:
Kuduro is Angolan electronic dance music built on driving electronic rhythms, distorted bass, and an associated acrobatic dance style. It developed in Luanda in the early 1990s, drawing on Miami bass, zouk, and electronic music circulating on cassette tapes. The word means 'hard ass' in Angolan Portuguese, referring to the dance's physically demanding, stiff-backed style.

Kuduro blew up internationally when it appeared in Fast and Furious films and through Buraka Som Sistema, a Portuguese collective who brought it to European clubs. MCA and Titica are major figures in the genre's local Angolan context. The music is raw, fast, and built for physical dancing.

name: Mbalax
description:
Mbalax is the popular music of Senegal and the Gambia, built around the sabar drum ensemble and the tama (talking drum) combined with electric guitar and bass, horns, and modern production. Youssou N'Dour created the definitive modern form in the 1970s and 80s by blending Wolof griot traditions with Cuban music and rock influences he had absorbed.

The sabar's complex rhythms, which are used in traditional Wolof ceremonies, give mbalax its rhythmic complexity and its connection to local cultural practice. The music is simultaneously modern pop and deeply traditional. N'Dour's international collaborations with Peter Gabriel and Neneh Cherry brought the sound to a global audience, but mbalax's primary audience remains in Senegal and the diaspora.

name: Chiptune
description:
Chiptune uses the sound synthesis chips from vintage video game consoles and home computers -- the Commodore 64's SID chip, the Game Boy's DMG sound hardware, the NES's 2A03 -- as musical instruments. The sounds are inherently limited: a handful of waveforms, limited polyphony, and a characteristic clean digital tone that is inseparable from 1980s gaming culture. Composers like Rob Hubbard and Ben Daglish made the C64 chip sing in ways that shaped a generation's relationship with electronic sound.

Chiptune became a deliberate aesthetic choice in the 2000s, when artists began using original hardware to make music for performance and recording rather than games. The 8bitpeoples collective in New York, Anamanaguchi's use of a hacked NES, and artists like Bit Shifter developed the form. Chiptune sits at the intersection of music and video game nostalgia.

name: Bedroom Pop
description:
Bedroom pop is made by one person recording alone with a laptop and basic equipment. The production is intentionally lo-fi -- there are room sounds, hiss, imperfect performances -- but the songs are melodic and emotionally direct. The aesthetic valorizes intimacy and imperfection as authentic in opposition to the polish of professional recording.

Although the term is recent, the practice is old: Elliott Smith made his first recordings this way; Daniel Johnston's cassette tapes were bedroom pop before the genre had a name. In the Spotify era, bedroom pop became a genre tag and a commercial category. Rex Orange County and Clairo built large audiences from bedroom recordings that retained their domestic texture even after they attracted label interest.

name: Hyperpop
description:
Hyperpop exaggerates every element of pop production to the point of distortion: the bass is too loud, the treble too sharp, the AutoTune more extreme, the tempo unstable. A.G. Cook and SOPHIE at PC Music in London developed the approach in the mid-2010s as a kind of pop art project, creating music that sounded like pop heard through a broken speaker. The emotional effect is simultaneously catchy and overwhelming.

Sophie's Object Blue and 100 Gecs each pushed the formula toward different extremes. The genre became closely associated with internet culture, gender non-conformity, and a generation that grew up surrounded by algorithmically maximized content. Hyperpop asks whether any sound is too extreme for pop if the hook is strong enough.

name: Folktronica
description:
Folktronica combines acoustic folk instrumentation with electronic production techniques. Computers and synthesizers process acoustic sounds, add textures, or provide rhythmic counterpoints to acoustic guitar, fiddle, or voice. The approach differs from ambient folk in that the electronic elements are often as prominent as the acoustic ones. Tunng and Four Tet pioneered different versions of the approach in the UK.

Four Tet's Kieran Hebden created fragile, intricate electronic music from folk and acoustic samples. Tunng built songs from acoustic instruments surrounded by found sounds and subtle electronic processing. The genre sits within a larger post-Bjork tendency to treat the acoustic and the electronic not as opposites but as complementary textures.
key_tracks: My Angel Rocks Back and Forth

name: Trip-Hop
description:
Trip-hop emerged from Bristol in the early 1990s as a slower, more cinematic version of hip-hop built on sampled breakbeats, jazz and soul samples, and a heavily textured production style. Massive Attack's Blue Lines in 1991 and Portishead's Dummy in 1994 defined the genre: sparse, atmospheric, deeply melancholy, with female vocals over beats that moved at the pace of late-night thought.

The name itself was slightly disparaged by the artists it described, who mostly called it simply electronic music or hip-hop. Tricky's paranoid, dense productions took it in a darker direction. The Bristol sound influenced a generation of electronic and alternative music and found a large mainstream audience despite its unhurried, inward-looking qualities.

name: Glam Metal
description:
Glam metal combined hard rock guitar riffs with the visual excess of glam rock: teased hair, spandex, leather, and enormous production budgets. The music was catchy, guitar-driven, and designed for FM radio and MTV. Motley Crue's 1981 debut Too Fast for Love and Ratt's debut established the Los Angeles template. The songs were about sex, partying, and the rock and roll lifestyle -- nothing more, nothing less.

At its commercial peak in the mid-to-late 1980s, bands like Poison, Bon Jovi, and Whitesnake dominated rock radio and MTV. Grunge's arrival in 1991 essentially ended glam metal's mainstream run almost overnight. The genre survived in a smaller nostalgic circuit and experienced periodic critical reassessment that recognized its songs were often more carefully crafted than contemporary critics allowed.

name: NWOBHM
description:
The New Wave of British Heavy Metal was a movement of the late 1970s and early 1980s that reinvigorated heavy metal by infusing it with punk's speed and DIY energy. Where Black Sabbath and Deep Purple had made metal slow and thunderous, NWOBHM bands played fast, tight, and with twin guitar harmonies inspired by Thin Lizzy. Iron Maiden and Judas Priest were its two primary architects.

The movement produced dozens of bands, most of them short-lived, through regional scenes in Birmingham, Sheffield, and the northeast. Motorhead's ferocious punk-metal hybrid was a parallel development. NWOBHM directly influenced thrash metal: Lars Ulrich formed Metallica after seeing Diamond Head in England and bringing their demo tapes back to California.

name: Crossover Thrash
description:
Crossover thrash is the literal meeting point between thrash metal and hardcore punk. D.R.I.'s 1985 album Crossover gave the genre its name. The music is faster than either parent genre, with short songs, downtuned guitars, and a shared intensity that hardcore and thrash had been approaching from different directions. The musicians and audiences were largely interchangeable between the two scenes.

Suicidal Tendencies, Nuclear Assault, and Municipal Waste each developed the crossover template with distinct regional flavors. The genre was genuinely underground and resisted the commercial polishing that affected both thrash and hardcore in the late 1980s. It has maintained a devoted following and periodic revivals.

name: Industrial Metal
description:
Industrial metal fused the aggression and production tools of industrial music with the guitar-driven structure of heavy metal. Ministry's Psalm 69 in 1992 is the genre's defining commercial statement: down-tuned guitars over sequenced industrial rhythms, samples, and Al Jourgensen's processed vocal delivery. Nine Inch Nails approached the same territory from a more electronic angle.

Rammstein in Germany took the format and made it stadium-sized, combining industrial metal riffs with operatic staging and provocative German-language lyrics. White Zombie and Rob Zombie added horror movie aesthetics. The genre has proven commercially durable because it combines metal's guitar catharsis with industrial's sonic aggression.

name: Post-Metal
description:
Post-metal applies post-rock's patience and dynamic range to heavy metal instrumentation. Songs are long, structured around slow builds and occasional massive releases of heavy guitar. Neurosis, who began as a hardcore band and gradually transformed their sound over several albums, are considered the genre's origin point. The music is meditative and heavy simultaneously, asking the same investment from the listener that post-rock does but with a darker tonal palette.

Isis and Pelican developed more accessible versions in the 2000s, while Cult of Luna and Russian Circles brought European and American perspectives. Post-metal has a devoted following among listeners who want metal's physical weight alongside the contemplative arc of instrumental rock.

name: Coldwave
description:
Coldwave is the French and Belgian post-punk variant that emphasized synthesizer minimalism, cold production, and an even more severe emotional distance than the British post-punk it developed alongside. Initiated by the French music press to describe their local post-punk scene, it included bands like Asylum Party and Martin Dupont who made bleaker, more electronic music than Joy Division but without the melodic relief.

The genre overlaps with but is distinct from darkwave: darkwave has more gothic romantic aesthetics, while coldwave is starker and more minimalist. It has experienced a revival through labels like Avant! Records reissuing French and Italian coldwave recordings and a new generation of producers making music in the tradition.

name: Retrowave
description:
Retrowave (also called synthwave or outrun) is a contemporary electronic genre built entirely from the sounds and aesthetics of 1980s synthesizer music, action film soundtracks, and video game music. Kavinsky's 2007 track 'Testarossa Autodrive' and the French electronic scene around Ed Banger Records helped establish the aesthetic. The music sounds like a lost 1984 film score.

Drive (2011) and Stranger Things gave retrowave a massive mainstream platform. Artists like Gunship, Perturbator, and Carpenter Brut made the aesthetic more aggressive and darker. The genre is explicitly nostalgic for a specific cultural moment that many of its producers are too young to have experienced firsthand, which gives it an interesting relationship to authentic retro production.

name: Indie Electronic
description:
Indie electronic blends the production techniques and aesthetic sensibility of independent electronic music with the song structures and guitar or vocal traditions of indie rock. Artists in this space use synthesizers and programming as core components rather than as decoration for rock arrangements. Passion Pit, MGMT, and Grizzly Bear (whose more electronic moments) explored this territory.

The genre encompasses a wide range, from the energetic dance pop of Passion Pit to the introspective synthesis of Neon Indian. What defines it is the indie aesthetic -- a valuing of texture over polish, emotional honesty over commercial calculation -- applied to electronic music's tools. Many indie electronic artists came from rock backgrounds and brought rock's song economy to electronic production.

name: Tropical House
description:
Tropical house is a lighter, warmer variant of house music characterized by flute and marimba-like synthesizer melodies, slower tempos, and a deliberately relaxed, beach-holiday atmosphere. Thomas Jack, an Australian producer, helped create and name the genre around 2013. The music strips out the intensity of conventional house in favor of a weightless, summer-vacation quality.

Kygo brought the format to a massive global audience with melodic piano and acoustic guitar elements added to the electronic production. Justin Bieber's 'Sorry' and Ed Sheeran's 'Shape of You' absorbed tropical house elements into mainstream pop production. The genre became one of the dominant sounds in commercial pop between 2015 and 2018.

name: Gypsy Jazz
description:
Gypsy jazz was created by Django Reinhardt and Stephane Grappelli in Paris in the early 1930s. It combines American swing jazz with Romani musical traditions, producing a sound that is unmistakably jazz but with a distinctive melodic and ornamental approach rooted in Eastern European folk music. Reinhardt played with two fingers on his fretting hand -- the others were paralyzed in a caravan fire -- and developed a unique technique that nobody has fully replicated.

The Quintette du Hot Club de France's recordings from the 1930s and 40s are the genre's founding documents. Gypsy jazz is one of the most distinctive and recognizable guitar styles in any genre. It has maintained a devoted international community of practitioners and audiences who study Reinhardt's vocabulary with near-academic rigor.
key_tracks: I'll See You in My Dreams

name: Latin Alternative
description:
Latin alternative encompasses rock and alternative music made in Spanish and Portuguese that combines indie rock aesthetics with Latin musical traditions. Bands from Argentina, Mexico, and Chile brought the sonic palette of 1980s alternative rock into dialogue with cumbia, bossa nova, and traditional folk from their regions. Soda Stereo from Argentina and Cafe Tacuba from Mexico were the genre's primary architects.

The genre resists simple classification because it spans such a large geographic and sonic range. It differs from Latin rock (which tends toward hard rock) in its indie aesthetic and willingness to experiment. The Latin Grammy Award for Best Alternative/Rock Album has helped institutionalize the category and its acts.

name: Cumbia Pop
description:
Cumbia pop blends the traditional Colombian cumbia rhythm with contemporary pop production, making the genre more accessible to urban youth audiences. The accordion and gaita flute of traditional cumbia are replaced or supplemented with synthesizers, and the arrangement is cleaned up for radio. Carlos Vives brought this approach to international audiences with his 1993 album Clasicos de la Provincia.

In Argentina, a distinct variant called cumbia villera developed in the poor urban barrios, with more aggressive social commentary. Mexican cumbia has its own commercial pop tradition. Shakira's early incorporation of cumbia rhythms into pop brought the rhythm to global audiences who would not have encountered traditional cumbia.

name: New Age
description:
New age music is designed for meditation, relaxation, and spiritual practice rather than active listening or social dancing. It draws from ambient music, classical minimalism, and world music traditions, using synthesizers, solo piano, acoustic guitar, and natural sounds to create an atmosphere of calm. Will Ackerman founded Windham Hill Records in 1976 and built the genre's primary commercial infrastructure.

Enya's synthesis of Celtic tradition with synthesizer production brought the aesthetic to a mainstream audience in the late 1980s. Andreas Vollenweider's harp work and Steven Halpern's overtone-tuned recordings each defined distinct corners of the genre. New age has always been adjacent to self-help culture, which has shaped both its audience and the skepticism with which critics have treated it.

name: Boogaloo
description:
Boogaloo was a New York dance music hybrid created by young Puerto Rican and Cuban musicians who grew up in the city and absorbed both their parents' Latin music and the Black American R&B around them. It had English lyrics, Latin rhythms, soul vocal style, and a deliberate bilingual and bicultural identity. Joe Cuba's 1966 track 'Bang Bang' and Pete Rodriguez's 'I Like It Like That' were its commercial peaks.

The genre was brief but significant: it lasted roughly from 1966 to 1969 before being superseded by salsa, which the older Latin music establishment considered more culturally authentic. Boogaloo's legacy is as a specifically American Latino form, born from New York's mixing of cultures, that had no exact equivalent in Latin America.

name: Choro
description:
Choro is Brazil's oldest urban music tradition, developed in Rio de Janeiro by musicians who absorbed European forms -- polka, waltz, habanera, schottische -- and played them with African rhythmic inflection and improvisation. The instrumentation centers on the flute, the 7-string guitar, and the pandeiro (frame drum), with the cavaquinho (small guitar) providing harmony. The music is fast, melodically complex, and demands technical precision.

Chiquinha Gonzaga and Ernesto Nazareth were early composers; Pixinguinha is the tradition's canonical figure. Choro went through a significant revival in the 1970s, when younger Brazilian musicians rediscovered it as an alternative to both bossa nova and rock. It remains an active tradition with a dedicated community of practitioners and regular rodas de choro (jam sessions) throughout Brazil.
